scenario_name: Scenario 21 — Multi-Parcel Tracking Partial Delivery Detection
justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Shipment vs Parcel clearly distinguished as aggregate root and child entity with separate lifecycles. (2) Shipment status explicitly called out as derived/projected from Parcel states in BR-4 ('never set independently'), AC-8 ('computed from Parcel statuses on read, not retrieved from a stored field'), and the ubiquitous language definition. (3) Eight parcel-level states defined (CREATED, PICKED_UP, IN_TRANSIT, OUT_FOR_DELIVERY, DELIVERED, FAILED_DELIVERY_ATTEMPT, LOST, RETURNED_TO_SENDER) plus DISPUTE_UNDER_REVIEW. (4) Business invariants thoroughly cover partial delivery detection (BR-6, BR-7), lost parcel escalation (BR-10, AC-3), and parcel-level dispute/refund (BR-9, BR-12). (5) Eight non-trivial edge cases surfaced including concurrent updates, migration, multi-dispute, reshipment, and all-lost scenarios. (6) Ten detailed acceptance criteria in Given/When/Then format.

observations.text: The derivation rule — the most critical domain insight — is stated emphatically in BR-4, AC-8, and the Shipment Status glossary entry, with explicit 'no setter' testable assertion.
Twelve business rules with invariant classification and justification for each — significantly exceeds the minimum expected depth.
Edge case for concurrent Parcel updates within the same Shipment directly addresses the race condition risk from the rubric.
Migration edge case (AC-9) shows awareness that this is a refactoring of an existing system, not greenfield — addresses backward compatibility.
Dispute granularity is thoroughly handled: BR-9 requires Parcel IDs on disputes, BR-12 defines the freeze behavior, and edge cases cover multi-Parcel disputes and single-Parcel Shipment disputes.
Business impact analysis with six stakeholder groups adds practical grounding beyond pure domain modeling.

The 'lost-then-found' edge case from the rubric is partially addressed (BR-10 rejects LOST->DELIVERED) but does not discuss the real-world scenario where a carrier locates a package after loss declaration — only blocks the transition without proposing a resolution path.

Time windows for partial delivery escalation (how long before a partially-delivered shipment triggers proactive customer outreach) are acknowledged as a low-confidence area but not explored as a business rule.

agent_output_excerpt:
Now I have full context. The codebase has no production logistics entities — this is a greenfield domain design. Let me produce the domain analysis.

{"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Refactoring a logistics platform from shipment-level tracking (one tracking number, one status per shipment) to parcel-level tracking.
